coredata.dc:title: A multifunctional guanosine-based carbon dots for dead microbial imaging and synergistic broad-spectrum antimicrobial therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Nong, Shuli
Wang, Mengru
Wang, Xuexiao
Li, Yongchun
Yu, Saiyin
Tang, Changhua
Li, Guanying
Xu, Li
coredata.dc:description: Pathogenic microbial infection poses serious threats to human lives and antibiotic abuse leads to bacterial resistance. The development of alternative antibacterial agents and strategies with reduced resistance risk is urgently demanded. Heteroatom doping is a feasible method for adjusting the electronic and optical properties of carbon dots (CDs) to improve their bioimaging performance and light-mediated therapeutic efficiency. Herein, we synthesized a series of guanosine-derived CDs from guanosine (G), guanosine monophosphate (GMP), guanosine diphosphate (GDP), and guanosine triphosphate (GTP) as respective precursors. G-CDs, prepared by guanosine with strong surface negative charge and suitable size, can successfully differentiate and stain dead microbial cells (bacteria and fungi). G-CDs with a high N content exhibited the highest antibacterial activity in vitro and in vivo among the four guanosine-derived CDs, owing to triple synergistic antibacterial actions - the intrinsic antibacterial mechanism, bacterial photodynamic and photothermal inactivation against bacteria and fungi. In this paper, we present a promising new method to prepare CDs using natural biomolecules from living systems as precursors for multifunctional dead microbial cells imaging and broad-spectrum theranostic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Guanosine-derived CDs
Broad-spectrum disinfection
Dead microbial cell imaging
Photodynamic
Photothermal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of guanosine-derived carbon dots
Live/dead bacteria and fungi differentiation and imaging
In vivo treatment of bacterially infected wounds

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangzhou
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Guangzhou Municipal Science and Technology Project

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Projects of Social Welfare and Basic Research of Zhongshan City

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Key Projects of Social Welfare and Basic Research of Zhongshan City ( 2021B2007 ), the Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangzhou (No. 202002030089 ), the National Science Foundation of China (No. 31971314 ), special funds of key disciplines construction from Guangdong and Zhongshan cooperating, and partially supported by the “Yong Talent Support Plan” of Xi’an Jiaotong University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) images of G-CDs. (c) ζ-potential of G-CDs, GMP-CDs, GDP-CDs and GTP-CDs. XPS full spectra (d) and high-resolution C 1 s (e), N 1 s (f), O 1 s (g) spectra of G-CDs. (h) FTIR spectra of Guanosine and G-CDs.

Photothermal and photodynamic properties of G-CDs. (a) Schematic illustration of the possible photophysically activated process of G-CDs. (b) Thermal image photographs for different concentrations of G-CDs solution under 808 nm (1.5 W cm−2) light irradiation for 5 min. (c, d) Photothermal curves of G-CDs solution with different concentrations under an 808 nm light irradiation (1.5 W cm−2, 10 min) and various 808 nm laser power densities at pH 7.4 at a concentration of 90 μg mL−1. (e) Heating and cooling curves of G-CDs solution (90 µg mL−1) for five on/off cycles (808 nm, 1.5 W cm−2). (f) Calculation of the photothermal-conversion efficiency (η) of G-CDs at 808 nm. Blue line: temperature rising and cooling profiles of G-CDs for certain periods. Red line: time constant (τs) from the cooling period using the linear time data. (g) 1O2 and (h) O2 •– assessments in the presence of G-CDs with different concentrations under 405 nm light irradiation (0.5 W cm−2).

(a) Confocal laser scanning microscope images of dead G+ (S. aureus), G− (E. coli), and fungi (C. albicans) treated with G-CDs solution (32 μg mL−1) at 37 °C for 1 h, λ ex = 405, 488 and 552 nm, respectively. Scale bar: 5 μm. (b) Flow cytometric results to analyze the fluorescence signals of live and dead S. aureus, E. coli, and C. albicans without (control) and with G-CDs solution (32 μg mL−1) for 1 h at 37 °C separately utilizing FITC, PE and PE-Texes Red three channels.

Agar plates photographs (a) and relative bacterial viability (b) of S. aureus treated without or with G-CDs (16 and 32 μg mL−1) under dark, 405 nm (0.5 W cm−2, 10 min, PDT) single light, 808 nm (1.5 W cm−2, 10 min, PTT) single light, and 405/808 nm (0.5 W cm−2, 1.5 W cm−2, 10 min, PDTT) dual light irradiation, respectively. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001). (c) Fluorescent live/dead assay of S. aureus with G-CDs (32 μg mL−1) under dark, 405 nm (0.5 W cm−2) single light and 405/808 nm (0.5 W cm−2, 1.5 W cm−2, 10 min) dual light irradiation. Live and dead bacteria separately exhibited green and red signals (scale bar: 5 μm). (d) SEM images of S. aureus incubated with/without G-CDs (64 μg mL−1) under dark, 405 nm (0.5 W cm−2, 10 min) single light and 405/808 nm (0.5 W cm−2, 1.5 W cm−2, 10 min) dual light irradiation (scale bar: 2 μm).

Antibacterial mechanisms investigation. (a-c) ATP level, (d-f) nucleic acid and (g-i) protein leakage level from S. aureus, E. coli, and C. albicans with various concentrations of G-CDs under dark, 405 nm (0.5 W cm−2, 10 min, PDT) single light, and 405/808 nm (0.5 W cm−2, 1.5 W cm−2, 10 min, PDTT) dual light irradiation, respectively. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3, n.s means no significance, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001).

In vivo synergistic antibacterial efficacy and wound healing assessment. (a) Schematic representation of S. aureus skin wound infection and treatment by G-CDs with 405/808 nm dual light irradiation. (b) Infrared thermal images of mice with G-CDs (0, 30, 60 and 90 μg mL−1) under 808 nm light irradiation. (c) Temperature elevation at the wound site of infected mice under 808 nm (1.5 W cm−2) light irradiation. (d) Representative photographs and (e) wound area of infected wounds at the indicated time points after different treatments. (f) Statistical analysis of bacterial counts obtained from the wound tissues. (g) Schematic diagram of wound contraction in different treatment groups.
H&E staining of the wound section in different treatment groups on days 1, 5 and 12. Scale bar: 100 μm.
Schematic illustration of guanosine-based carbon dots synthesis and their applications in dead microorganisms imaging and broad-spectrum microorganisms therapy.

head.ce:title: A multifunctional guanosine-based carbon dots for dead microbial imaging and synergistic broad-spectrum antimicrobial therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biophysics, School of Basic Medical Sciences, Health Science Centre, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Shaanxi, 710061, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Zhongshan, 528458, PR China (L. Xu)

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pathogenic microbial infection poses serious threats to human lives and antibiotic abuse leads to bacterial resistance. The development of alternative antibacterial agents and strategies with reduced resistance risk is urgently demanded. Heteroatom doping is a feasible method for adjusting the electronic and optical properties of carbon dots (CDs) to improve their bioimaging performance and light-mediated therapeutic efficiency. Herein, we synthesized a series of guanosine-derived CDs from guanosine (G), guanosine monophosphate (GMP), guanosine diphosphate (GDP), and guanosine triphosphate (GTP) as respective precursors. G-CDs, prepared by guanosine with strong surface negative charge and suitable size, can successfully differentiate and stain dead microbial cells (bacteria and fungi). G-CDs with a high N content exhibited the highest antibacterial activity in vitro and in vivo among the four guanosine-derived CDs, owing to triple synergistic antibacterial actions - the intrinsic antibacterial mechanism, bacterial photodynamic and photothermal inactivation against bacteria and fungi. In this paper, we present a promising new method to prepare CDs using natural biomolecules from living systems as precursors for multifunctional dead microbial cells imaging and broad-spectrum theranostic applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Microbiological infections with high morbidity and mortality are one of the most prominent public health problems [1,2]. A commonly used approach to treating bacterial infections involves administering antibiotics [3]. However, inappropriate or excessive use of antibiotics can easily lead to bacterial resistance against antibiotics, reducing the efficacy of antibiotic therapy [4]. Thus, alternative therapeutic strategies and antibacterial agents able to circumvent antibiotic resistance are urgently needed [5].

Because of its noninvasive nature, highly temporal and spatial selectivity, and the ability to overcome resistance, phototherapy, such as photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT), is an excellent alternative to antibiotics [6,7]. PTT takes advantage of photothermal agents to absorb light with the appropriate wavelength and then convert photon energy into heat, elevating their surface temperature to produce local hyperthermia, which can destroy cell membranes, denature bacterial proteins, and cause the death of bacteria [4]. While PDT uses photosensitizers (PSs) to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS, including 1O2, OH•, O2 •–) to kill microbial cells through electron transfer or energy transfer [7]. However, single-mode phototherapy commonly needs high heat (greater than 70 °C) or excessive ROS to kill microorganisms, respectively, which may damage or inflame nearby healthy tissues [8]. Recently, dual-mode phototherapy (PTT and PDT) can reduce antibacterial time and dose, produce a synergistic effect and enhance antibacterial effectiveness [9–12]. Thus, the development of a novel and simple phototherapeutic agent that takes advantage of dual-mode phototherapy is urgently needed for treating bacterial infections.

As a new kind of optical nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) show excellent biocompatibility, high stability, water solubility, low toxicity, and unique optical properties, which have been widely employed in bioimaging, biosensing, drug/gene delivery, antibacterial applications, and anticancer treatments [13–15]. In order to improve CDs' optical imaging performance and light-mediated therapeutic efficiency, heteroatom doping is an effective method of modulating their electronic structure and optical properties [16]. As an example, nitrogen (N) is considered a good dopant because of its similar atomic size to carbon, its strong electronegativity, and its presence of five valence electrons that aid in chemical bonding, as well as a lone pair of electrons that can be easily transferred to the π-orbitals of sp2 carbon structures. Thus, N-doping can regulate structural defects and enhance optical properties of CDs [17,18]. In addition, N-doping can also form various N-based chemical structures such as pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, resulting in the formation of diverse structures and sometimes unique characteristics in CDs [19]. A series of papers reported N-doped CDs for biosensing, bioimaging, therapeutics, and methods for synthesizing them [20–22]. Especially, ribonucleoside possessing high biological abandance and rich nitrogen contents have been widely adopted as precursors to prepare eco-friendly CDs, findng interesting applications in sensing, bioimaging and catalysis [23–26]. For example, adenosine and its phosphorylated analogous-derived CDs exhibited excellent photophysical properties and low cytotoxicities against mammalian cells, making them promissing candidates as phototherapeutic agents for treating diseases beyond imaging [24]. However, to the best of our knowledge, ribonucleoside-derived CDs employed for simultaneous dead microbial cell imaging and broad-spectrum inactivation are still lacking.

Guanosine, compromising guanine as the nuclear base that has the lowest redox potential, represents the most vulnerable one among the nucleosides, and is therefore the most easily oxidized [27]. We speculate that guanosine-derived CD remains the redox activity, capable to convert the light irradiation to reactive speciese for phototherapy. In this regard, we select guanosine as a precursor to prepare guanosine-based CD through one-step hydrothermal carbonization for light-mediated disinfection. Moreover, phosphorus, belonging to group fifteen of the periodic table and being larger than carbon, it can act as an n-type donor, causing substitutional defects in the carbon cluster and thus altering the electronic and optical properties of CDs [16]. To investigate the roles of P-doping on the excited state engineering of CDs, we also synthesized N, P-doped CDs using guanosine monophosphate (GMP), guanosine diphosphate (GDP), and guanosine triphosphate (GTP) as respective precursors. Their photophysical and photochemical properties including photoluminescence quantum yields, photothermal conversion efficiencies, and photo-induced reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation were compared. Due to their excellent photophysical properties, these four CDs showed high potents in staining dead microbial cell. Finally, their capability for broad-spectrum disinfection are verified. Among them, G-CDs with a high N content exhibited the strongest antimicrobial activity in vitro and in vivo antimicrobial experiments against S. aureus, E. coli, and C. albicans upon dual light irradiation owing to the combined effect of chemical therapy, photodynamic and photothermal therapy (Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of guanosine-derived carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Four types of guanosine-derived CDs, namely G-CDs, GMP-CDs, GDP-CDs, and GTP-CDs, were synthesized through a straightforward method using guanosine (G), guanosine monophosphate (GMP), guanosine diphosphate (GDP), and guanosine triphosphate (GTP) as respective precursors. The supporting information provided detailed preparation procedures. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images revealed that all four synthesized CDs were monodisperse spheres with diameters of ≈5.1 nm, ≈4.5 nm, ≈5.0 nm, and ≈6.0 nm, respectively. This indicated 2D structures were in accordance with the typical morphology of graphitic nanoplatelets (Fig. 1 a, Fig. S1b). G-CDs had a lattice spacing of 0.24 nm, corresponding to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1b) [28]. GDP-CDs showed fluorescence excitation and emission peaks at 348 nm and 452 nm, respectively. G-CDs, GMP-CDs, and GTP-CDs had similar fluorescence excitation and emission spectra with peaks at 325 ± 5 nm and 385 ± 5 nm, respectively (Fig. S2e–h, Table S1). Moreover, all CDs had excitation wavelength-dependent emission spectra (Fig. S2a–d). This unique feature shows that CDs possess a variety of localized emissive states within a single particle. They can form multiple photoluminescence bands ascribed to functional groups on surfaces and edges, graphitic nanoclusters, and molecular fluorophores. The quantum yield (QY) values were 14.52 %, 22.71 %, 9.69 % and 9.95 % for G-CDs, GMP-CDs, GDP-CDs and GTP-CDs, respectively (Table S1). Time-resolved fluorescence spectra of the four CDs were recorded (Fig. S4e) and the average fluorescence lifetimes were obtained after fitting the photoluminescence decay curve with a double-exponential function (Table S2). The average fluorescence lifetimes of G-CDs, GMP-CDs, GDP-CDs and GTP-CDs were 6.15 ns, 9.58 ns, 5.03 ns and 7.33 ns, respectively. Additionally, the wide absorption range between 600 − 850 nm from visible to NIR was ascribed to the heavily N-doped and O-containing functional groups at the edges of G-CDs (Fig. S4a) [29]. The ζ-potential results showed that our CDs varied in surface charge and G-CDs showed the highest negative potential of −14.5 mV. This could be attributed to the presence of carboxyl groups on the surface [25]. With the decrease in nitrogen content, the surface charge of CDs (produced from G to GTP) became less negatively charged and reached saturation with ζ-potential values of −14.5 mV, −12.1 mV, 0.59 mV, and 0.32 mV, respectively (Fig. 1c). The elements on four CDs were confirmed by full-scan X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. 1d, Fig. S1a) and the high resolution C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and P 2p XPS spectra of four CDs were also collected (Fig. 1e–g, Fig. S3). G-CDs had the highest nitrogen content (Fig. 1d), and the high resolution C1s spectrum comprised C = O, C–O, C–N, C–C, and C = C peaks at 288.2, 286.2, 285.7, 284.9 and 284.3 eV, respectively (Fig. 1e) [23]. A high resolution N1s spectrum unveiled peaks at 400.6, 399.8, and 398.9 eV, separately indicating C–N, N–H, and C = N–H (Fig. 1f) [23]. The high resolution O1s spectrum revealed O–C = O (533.6 eV), C–O–C/C–OH (531.8 eV) and O2– (530.4 eV) (Fig. 1g) [30]. The high resolution C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s XPS spectra for GMP-CDs, GDP-CDs and GTP-CDs were similar to G-CDs. A new peak at ≈535.1 eV in O1s XPS spectra of GMP-CDs might be physically adsorbed by H2O and O2 [24]. In the high resolution P 2p XPS spectra of GMP-CDs, GDP-CDs and GTP-CDs, two main peaks P-O and P = O were observed (Fig. S3) [23]. In addition, the nitrogen contents of source materials were also calculated based on the molecular weight to be G (24.7 %), GMP (17.2 %), GDP (15.8 %), and GTP (13.4 %). In combination with XPS analysis results, the final CDs might be greatly affected by the nitrogen content of substrates. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were used to compare G-CDs with G, which offered detailed chemical information, and some differences between G-CDs and G are observed (Fig. 1h). G-CDs exhibited typical peaks at 1358, 1602, 1690, 3134, and 3423 cm−1 representing − COO symmetric stretching vibrations, C = C/ C = O stretching modes, N − H, and O − H. The bands at 619 cm−1 and 1143 cm−1 were separately attributed to C–H bending vibration and C–O stretching vibration. Therefore, the hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups are the main functional groups on the surface of G-CDs [31,32]. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) of G-CDs unveiled a broad (002) peak at 24.5°, further indicating the graphene structure of G-CDs (Fig. S4b) [33]. G-CDs samples remained clear and transparent after 14 days at room temperature (Fig. S4c) and exhibited constant luminescence characteristics under UV irradiation (Fig. S4d), indicating that G-CDs are highly stable.

To further clarify the exciton dynamics of G-CDs, we performed femtosecond transient absorption (TA) experiments under 320 nm excitation. A positive and negative signal can be observed from 2D false color maps of the TAS data matrix (Fig. S4f). To investigate the detailed process, the TA of G-CDs at different delay times was shown in Fig. S4g. There was an obviously negative signal at around 400 nm, which matched the steady-state emission spectra, indicating that the peak was attributed to stimulated emission (SE) [34]. The positive signals (appearing in the regions of 360 − 390 and 413 − 430 nm) came from excited-state absorption (ESA) [35]. A double exponential fit of the SE kinetic traces at 406 nm was performed for G-CDs (Fig. S4h), with fits of τ1 = 32.22 ps and τ2 = 541.57 ps. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels of G-CDs were estimated by ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS). The fermi edge and end edge were determined as 19.87 eV and 4.39 eV, respectively (Fig. S5a) [36]. We arranged photon energy (hv) as the horizontal axis and (αhv)2 (α is the intensity of absorption) as the vertical axis to replot the UV–vis absorption curve. The band gap of G-CDs can be calculated to be 4.17 eV by the tangent method, corresponding to the photoluminescence emission peak of 400 nm (Fig. S5b). Then, the energy level of the HOMO could be calculated to be − 5.74 eV (The formula is –[21.22 eV−(19.87 − 4.39)] eV = − 5.74 eV, where 21.22 eV was the energy of He I). The energy level of the LUMO can be determined through the same method, while the formula was changed to −(5.74 − 4.17) eV = − 1.57 eV [37,38].

Fig. 2 a illustrates the potential energy transition pathway in CDs upon light exposure. Upon excitation from the ground state (S0) to the singlet state (S1), the excited electrons undergo radiative relaxation (fluorescence), nonradiative relaxation (heat production), and intersystem crossing to the triplet state (T1) for the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [39,40]. To validate the nonradiative relaxation pathway in CDs, we systematically investigated the photothermal attributes of four CDs under 808 nm laser irradiation based on their enhanced NIR absorption ability. G-CDs with more N content had the highest temperature elevation (Fig. S5c). This could be due to N-induced intragap states forming as the N content increased, and it played a crucial role in the generation of phonons (heat) via Shockley-Read-Hall electron-hole recombination or other defect-related pathways [19]. The photothermal conversion efficiencies (η) of four CDs were evaluated (Fig. 2f, Table S1). Among the four CDs, G-CDs had the highest η value (46.46 %), indicating that G-CDs can convert NIR energy into thermal energy rapidly and efficiently. We chose G-CDs as an example to assess their photothermal conversion performance under 808 nm laser irradiation because of their remarkable photothermal effect. The resulting temperature elevation exhibited concentration- and laser power-dependent characteristics (Fig. 2c, d). Infrared thermal camera visually demonstrated the temperature rise under 808 nm laser irradiation (Fig. 2b). There was no obvious change in the temperature after five cycles of 808 nm laser light ON/OFF procedures (Fig. 2e), which demonstrated that G-CDs displayed good photothermal stability. In addition, G-CDs irradiated with dual light (405/808 nm) showed a similar photothermal curve as with sole 808 nm irradiation (Fig. S5d).

Subsequently, the photodynamic properties of four CDs under 405 nm light irradiation were comprehensively assessed by measuring the total ROS (OH•, 1O2 and O2 •–) generation by 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein (DCF) assay [41]. Under 405 nm laser light (0.5 W cm−2), four CDs (32 μg mL−1) exhibited robust, time-dependent DCF fluorescence enhancements. Notably, G-CDs produced the highest fluorescence intensity, implying the largest amount of ROS production (Fig. S5e). In contrast, the untreated 2,7-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) solution showed negligible fluorescence alteration under 405 nm laser light irradiation. Next, Singlet Oxygen Sensor Green (SOSG) [42], Terephthalic Acid [43], and Micro Superoxide Anion Assay Kit [42] were separately used to identify the 1O2, OH•, and O2 •– production. G-CDs exhibited substantial, time-dependent and concentration-dependent enhancements in the SOSG- based emission and superoxide anion absorbance upon 405 nm (0.5 W cm−2) laser irradiation (Fig. 2g–h), demonstrating the abundant generation of 1O2 and O2 •–. Fig. S5f indicated no OH• production. In addition, no ROS generation occurred when an 808 nm (1.5 W cm−2) laser served as the light source. The fluorescence intensity trends of ROS for dual light irradiation (405/808 nm) resembled those of 405 nm light irradiation, indicating that only 405 nm light induces ROS production (Fig. S5g). 1O2 QY values were determined to be 0.31, 0.20, 0.23 and 0.19 for G-CDs, GMP-CDs, GDP-CDs and GTP-CDs, respectively (Table S1). G-CDs showed a higher 1O2 QY than the other three CDs. This might be attributed to the higher graphitic nitrogen content of G-CDs, which reduced the energy difference between the excited singlet state and the excited triplet state resulting in an increase in 1O2 generation [40]. Collectively, among the four CDs, G-CDs showed the best photothermal effects and the highest photoinduced ROS generation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Live/dead bacteria and fungi differentiation and imaging
ce:section.ce:para: We found that these four CDs could not stain live microorganisms due to electrostatic repulsion or low electrostatic attraction (Fig. S6a) [44,45]. While for dead microorganisms, red, green, and blue emission colors could be visualized under different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 3 a), indicating that G-CDs can selectively stain dead microorganisms. A dead cell marker, propidium iodide (PI) was then utilized to demonstrate the dead cell staining behavior of G-CDs. A merged confocal microscopic image showed strong yellow fluorescence signals, indicating that green G-CDs and red PI colocalized well and G-CDs can visualize dead cells (Fig. S6b). Flow cytometry analysis further confirmed these differentiations, with significantly enhanced fluorescent signals observed for dead microorganisms upon incubation with G-CDs (Fig. 3b).

Firstly, the minimal inhibitory concentrations (MICs) and minimum bactericidal concentrations (MBCs) of four CDs without light irradiation against S. aureus, E. coli and C. albicans were assessed and presented in Table S3 and Table S4, respectively. G-CDs displayed the best antibacterial activity among the four CDs against S. aureus (MICs = 32 μg mL−1), E. coli (MICs = 64 μg mL−1) and C. albicans (MICs = 16 μg mL−1), respectively. In addition, G-CDs were regarded as bactericidal agents against S. aureus, E. coli and C. albicans since their MBCs were no more than four times their MICs [46]. On account of the excellent inherent antibacterial activity, the high ROS generation and good photothermal conversion of G-CDs, the triple synergistic antimicrobial performances against S. aureus, E. coli and C. albicans were systematically investigated. The MIC values for G-CDs upon 10 min 405/808 nm (PDT/PTT means PTDT) dual light irradiation were 1/16, 1/32, and 1/16 of those for the dark condition of S. aureus, E. coli, and C. albicans, respectively, indicating the synergistic PDT and PTT antibacterial effect (Table S5).

A standard colony counting method was further applied to examine the synergistic antibacterial effects of G-CDs through combined PDT and PTT against S. aureus, E. coli, and C. albicans. The absence of G-CDs resulted in negligible antibacterial activity, indicating that 405 nm light alone for PDT, or the combination of 405/808 nm dual light irradiation for PTDT could not kill these microorganisms. For S. aureus, 32 μg mL−1 G-CDs exhibited 75 %, 93.4 %, 90 % and 99.95 % antibacterial efficacy in the dark, PDT, PTT and PTDT groups, respectively, indicating that a rapid and effective antibacterial performance was achieved by the synergistic PDT and PTT therapy (Fig. 4 a–b). Under 10 min of 405/808 nm light irradiation, the synergistic antibacterial effect with > 99.9 % sterilization rates could also be obtained for E. coli and C. albicans after separately treating with 64 and 16 μg mL−1 G-CDs (Fig. S7a–b and S8a-b). The different bactericide abilities of G-CDs against these three kinds of microbes could be observed: C. albicans > S. aureus > E. coli.

G-CDsʼ antibacterial properties were further evaluated by a live/dead staining assay after various treatments with SYTO 9 and PI dual fluorescent dyes. SYTO 9 labels all bacterial cells by binding to their DNA and RNA, emitting a green signal regardless of membrane integrity. On the other hand, PI selectively stains dead bacteria with damaged membranes, emitting a red signal [47]. Under 405/808 nm dual light irradiation without G-CDs, S. aureus, E. coli and C. albicans cells exhibited green fluorescence, indicating their viability. However, upon exposure to G-CDs or G-CDs with single light irradiation, a red fluorescence signal emerged, suggesting a specific antibacterial effect. Notably, when subjected to 405/808 nm dual light irradiation, almost all S. aureus, E. coli and C. albicans cells exhibited red fluorescence after incubation with G-CDs, indicating that G-CDs had enhanced toxicity in combined PDT/PTT (Fig. 4c, Fig. S7c and S8c).

SEM imaging was used to observe morphological changes in S. aureus (Fig. 4d), E. coli (Fig. S7d), and C. albicans (Fig. S8d) before and after treatment. In control with or without light irradiation, bacterial and fungal cells retained intact cell walls and smooth surfaces. However, a certain degree of collapse and damage was observed with G-CDs treatment. When S. aureus, E. coli, and C. albicans were exposed to G-CDs treatment under 405/808 nm dual light irradiation, G-CDs induced the most serious damage with abnormal membrane folding and surface fusion, indicating a stronger antibacterial ability originated from the synergistic effect.

To explore whether G-CDs induce bacterial and fungal resistance, G-CDs were treated with S. aureus, E. coli, and C. albicans at sub-inhibitory concentrations to detect MIC values for 25 days. Levofloxacin hydrochloride, a broad-spectrum fluoroquinolone, used as a positive control. The MIC of Levofloxacin began to rise on the seventh, eleventh, and seventeenth day, and increased up to 45, 100, and 36 folds on the 25th day against S. aureus, E. coli, and C. albicans, respectively. However, the MIC values of G-CDs did not notably change over 25 days (Fig. S9a–c), suggesting that bacteria and fungi did not easily develop resistance to G-CDs.

Using 3,3′-dipropylthiadicarbocyanine iodide (DiSC3(5)), a membrane potential-sensitive cyanine dye, cytoplasmic membrane depolarization can be monitored by concentrating in healthy polarized cytoplasmic membranes and causing self-quenching. In response to increased membrane permeability, depolarization occurs, resulting in a rise in fluorescence intensity [48]. G-CDs induced enhancements in DiSC3(5) fluorescence against S. aureus, E. coli, and C. albicans in dose-independent action (Fig. S9d–f), demonstrating that G-CDs effectively depolarized the microbial membranes.

PI dye was used to test the damage ability of G-CDs to the microbial cells inner membrane. PI can pass through damaged membranes, bind to the cellular DNA, and then emit fluorescence [49]. G-CDs displayed concentration-dependent increase in PI fluorescence signal against S. aureus, E. coli, and C. albicans (Fig. S9g–i), indicating that G-CDs could disrupte the integrity of microbial cell membrane.

Bacterial respiratory dehydrogenase, a membrane-bound enzyme, is an essential component of the bacterial respiratory chain, and participates in bacterial respiratory metabolism. Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) is such an enzyme and plays a vital role in the maintenance of redox balance inside cells [50]. The effects of G-CDs with dual light irradiation regulated membrane damage on the biological function of these membrane-bound enzymes were assessed by detecting LDH activity (Fig. S10a–b). Upon 256 μg mL−1 G-CDs treatment, S. aureus and E. coli exhibited 33 % and 43 % reductions in LDH activity, respectively. Notably, dual light irradiation caused an 83 % decrease in LDH activity in S. aureus and E. coli when treated with 256 μg mL−1 G-CDs. G-CDs exhibited extensive membrane damage under dual light irradiation. This might result in the separation of respiratory dehydrogenase from the bacterial membrane, causing LDH inactivation.

Adenosine triphosphate (ATP), an intracellular energy currency, is pivotal in cellular metabolism, respiration, and enzymatic reactions [51]. Monitoring microbial cellular ATP consumption provides insight into the impact of energy uncoupling, indicating the potential interference of G-CDs with cellular metabolism. Therefore, the effects of G-CDs, both alone and under dual light irradiation, on bacterial or fungal ATP levels were investigated (Fig. 5 a–c). G-CDs in S. aureus, E. coli and C. albicans exhibited good ATP depletion activity in a concentration-dependent manner. G-CDs with dual light irradiation exhibited higher ATP depletion effects than G-CDs without or with single light irradiation against S. aureus, E. coli and C. albicans.

When LDH is inactivated, metabolic inhibition occurs, and metabolism regulates organismic basic movements, which affects bacteria survival rate [52]. The effects of G-CDs on the cellular metabolic activity of S. aureus and E. coli were further evaluated by the Alamar Blue assay (Fig. S10c–d) [53]. G-CDs exhibited a gradual reduction in metabolic activity at increased concentrations. Moreover, 128 μg mL−1 G-CDs with 405/808 nm dual light irradiation for 10 min demonstrated more significant metabolic activity inactivation than those with the dark condition or individual 405 nm light irradiation against S. aureus and E. coli. These results indicate that G-CDs with 405/808 nm dual light irradiation damaged bacterial membrane integrity and hampered cellular respiration by inhibiting respiratory dehydrogenase, causing loss of bacterial viability and metabolic arrest.

Following the successful evaluation of extracellular ROS generation by G-CDs, we examined endogenous ROS production in microorganisms using the fluorescent probe 2,7-dichlorofluoroscein diacetate (DCFH-DA) [54]. Endogenous ROS levels induced by G-CDs under 405 nm light irradiation were quantified by flow cytometry (Fig. S10e–g). Relative to the control groups, G-CDs exhibited a remarkable augmentation in ROS levels upon 405 nm light irradiation for S. aureus, E. coli and C. albicans. This indicates G-CDs exhibited superior photodynamic inactivation properties in S. aureus, E. coli and C. albicans under 405 nm light irradiation. Consequently, endogenous ROS production was considered one of the main driving forces for killing bacteria.

In order to understand how oxidative stress affects bacterial viability, glutathione (GSH) depletion was monitored. GSH, a thiol-containing tripeptide, is pivotal in protecting cells against oxidative stress-induced damage, which plays a vital role in bacterial protective system through its antioxidative action [55]. G-CDs exhibited more pronounced GSH consumption under dual light irradiation (Fig. S10h). This suggests that G-CDs exposed to 405/808 nm dual light irradiation could accelerate GSH oxidation, disrupt intracellular antioxidant defenses and cause bacterial death.

Nucleic acids and proteins leak from microbial cells when the membrane ruptures. G-CDs can cause nucleic acids (Fig. 5d–f) and proteins leakage (Fig. 5g–i) in S. aureus, E. coli and C. albicans in a concentration-dependent manner. Relative to the control, 64 μg mL−1 G-CDs induced 7, 3 and 19-fold enhancements in nucleic acid leakage in S. aureus, E. coli and C. albicans, respectively. For S. aureus, 64 μg mL−1 G-CDs under dual light irradiation and 405 nm single light irradiation caused approximate 1.5 and 1.2-fold increments in bacterial nucleic acid leakage compared to the dark condition, with nucleic acid leakage concentrations up to 6.13 and 4.67 ng μL−1, respectively. Hence, G-CDs with dual light (PDTT) treatment resulted in the largest amount of nucleic acid leakage, suggesting the most serious bacteria destruction. Similar results were also observed in E. coli and C. albicans. We further detected protein leakage from microorganisms after different treatments through a BCA protein assay. The protein leakage level in G-CDs with dual light irradiation was higher than in the other groups against S. aureus, E. coli and C. albicans. In general, under dual light irradiation, G-CDs produce ROS and photothermal effects, causing bacterial membrane damage, and maximum contents leakage. On the other hand, G-CDs exposed to dual light irradiation inhibited membrane-bound respiratory dehydrogenases activity and disrupted the respiratory chain of bacteria, which further caused metabolic activity inactivation, and ATP levels reduction.

The biocompatibility of G-CDs was assessed through cell cytotoxicity and hemolytic activity. G-CDs displayed more than 80 % cell cytotoxicity to HepG2 and L02 cells at a concentration up to 200 μg mL−1. This concentration is higher than the highest MIC value for the tested bacteria (Fig. S11a–b). Thus, G-CDs did not cause marked toxicity to HepG2 and L02 cells. Hemolysis results indicated that 300 μg mL−1 G-CDs did not induce significant hemolytic activity against 2 % rabbit red blood cells (Fig. S11c). All these findings demonstrate the favorable biocompatibility of G-CDs at the given concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo treatment of bacterially infected wounds
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate CDsʼ antibacterial efficiencies and wound healing abilities, we utilized a full-thickness skin wound model infected by S. aureus and illuminated with light (Fig. 6 a). Levofloxacin was selected for comparison. During an 808 nm irradiation treatment, the temperature elevation exhibited a concentration-dependent characteristics in wound site, and 90 μg mL−1 G-CDs elevated to 55 °C after 3 min 405/808 nm dual light irradiation, indicating that G-CDs had a robust temperature rise in vivo (Fig. 6b and c). Fig. 6d and g were representative wound images and schematic illustrations depicting wound healing progression in the five treatment groups on days 0, 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, and 12. After 5 days treatment, G-CDs with 405/808 nm dual light irradiation exhibited superior wound recovery compared to the other four groups. This was indicated by the smallest wound area and the most pronounced bactericidal effect after 5 days. Notably, G-CDs with the dual light-treated group achieved almost complete healing after 11 day. Quantitative analysis indicated that the wound area reduction percentages on the 7th day were 90.0 % for the G-CDs/light group, 84 % for the Levofloxacin group, 81 % for the G-CDs group, 61 % for the control group and 55 % for the control/light group (Fig. 6e). In addition, the antibacterial therapeutic efficacy of G-CDs with a dual light-treated group was further quantitatively evaluated by analyzing bacterial counts in homogenized tissue dispersions from mice wounds. The dual light-treated G-CDs exhibited reduced bacterial colonization compared to G-CDs at day 5, illustrating the enhanced therapeutic effect of G-CDs under dual light irradiation (Fig. 6f and Fig. S12a).

Histological examination of wound skin tissues was carried out on days 1, 5 and 12 using Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) and Masson staining techniques (Fig. 7 and Fig. S13). On day 1, epidermal defects and infiltration of inflammatory cells caused by S. aureus infection were monitored in the wound tissues of all groups. On day 5, the control and control/light groups demonstrated a substantial presence of inflammatory cells. In contrast, the G-CDs/light and levofloxacin groups exhibited lower inflammatory cell infiltration levels than the control and control/light groups. On day 12, the wounds had not fully healed and epidermis damage appeared in the control and control/light groups. In contrast, the G-CDs/light, and levofloxacin groups displayed well-defined and intact epidermis. New hair follicles and blood vessels were observed in wound tissues treated with G-CDs/light and levofloxacin. Meanwhile, reduced inflammatory cells were found in the skin tissues of the G-CDs/light group. These results suggested that the G-CDs/light group promoted highly efficient wound healing because of effective antibacterial activity. In addition, collagen fibers appear as a blue color in the Masson staining. The G-CDs/light group displayed a notably bluer color and a significantly greater abundance of collagen fibers than the other groups (Fig. S13).

To further ensure G-CDs’ biosafety, we detected the body weight of the infected mice during the experiment, and all groups exhibited consistent and stable weight patterns (Fig. S12b). On the 12th day, no significant differences in organ to body weight ratios were observed in all treatment groups (Fig. S12c). Additionally, after 12 days of treatment, H&E-stained sections of major organs, including the heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys, revealed no distinguishable organ damage or pathological abnormalities for all groups (Fig. S14). The above results indicated that G-CDs exhibited reliable biosafety for eliminating pathogenic infections.

In this work, we successfully synthesized four CDs from guanosine (G), guanosine monophosphate (GMP), guanosine diphosphate (GDP), and guanosine triphosphate (GTP) as respective precursors. The optical properties of these guanosine-derived CDs were compared. Low content of P-doping increases the photoluminescence quantum yield, in line with literature findings [56]. High content of P-doping, however, remarkably reduced their photoluminescence quantum yield. Moreover, compared with G-CD, P-doping restrained the efficacy of photo-induced ROS generation and photothermal conversion efficiency, suggesting the suppressing role of P-dopant on the excited energy conversion of CDs. This could be probably due to P-doping-induced substitutional defects in the carbon cluster structure of CDs. Based on these outstanding optical properties, as well as the distinctive surface functional groups, suitable size and negative charge characteristic, G-CDs selectively stain dead microbes rather than living ones. Relative to GMP-CDs, GDP-CDs, and GTP-CDs, G-CDs with the highest N content exhibited the most effective antibacterial activity both in vitro and in vivo, owing to their exceptional triple synergistic antibacterial actions against bacteria and fungi through their inherent antibacterial mechanism, photodynamic and photothermal inactivation. G-CDs with dual light irradiation caused significant bacterial membrane disruption, leading to intracellular nucleic acids and proteins leakage. Moreover, G-CDs disrupted bacterial respiratory chain after dual light irradiation by inactivating membrane-bound respiratory dehydrogenases, further impairing metabolic activity and reducing ATP levels in bacteria. Furthermore, G-CDs consumed GSH under dual light irradiation, breaking intracellular antioxidant defenses. G-CDs exhibited minimal toxicity to human cells and significantly facilitating the healing of bacteria-infected wounds. In this study, we demonstrate how the synergistic combination of multiple therapeutic mechanisms enhances material-based antimicrobial efficacy, opening new anvenue for combatting bacterial infections.

Shuli Nong: Project administration, Methodology, Investigation. Mengru Wang: Project administration, Methodology, Investigation. Xuexiao Wang: Visualization, Methodology. Yongchun Li: Visualization, Methodology, Formal analysis. Saiyin Yu: Visualization, Methodology. Changhua Tang: Methodology. Guanying Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Li Xu: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Key Projects of Social Welfare and Basic Research of Zhongshan City (2021B2007), the Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangzhou (No. 202002030089), the National Science Foundation of China (No. 31971314), special funds of key disciplines construction from Guangdong and Zhongshan cooperating, and partially supported by the “Yong Talent Support Plan” of Xi’an Jiaotong University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.150123.

ce:section.ce:para:
